name: Salesforce
researcher_notes:
1) Auth methods (how the API authenticates)
- Salesforce REST API uses OAuth 2.0, either via an **external client app** or a **connected app**; requests must be authorized to access REST API resources. https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_oauth_and_connected_apps.htm
- To successfully send REST API requests, you need an **access token obtained by authentication** (the docs’ Quick Start walks through this). https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/quickstart.htm
- The docs also note you must pass an access token as a **Bearer token** for REST calls (example appears in resource pages like “Delete Records Using sObject Relationships”). https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/resources_sobject_relationships_delete.htm

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- Salesforce Developer Edition is explicitly described as **free** for testing/development, and the REST API Quick Start uses it. https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/quickstart.htm
- Creating connected apps is **restricted as of Spring ’26**; you can continue using existing connected apps, but for new ones the docs say you must “contact Salesforce Support” (or use external client apps instead). https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_rest.meta/api_rest/intro_oauth_and_connected_apps.htm
- Connected apps are created by Salesforce admins in a Salesforce org (i.e., credentials are not something you “self-generate” anonymously from the public site). https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/platform/mobile-sdk/guide/connected-apps-howto.html

3) Public API surface breadth + existing MCP server
- REST API surface: Salesforce’s REST API is very broad, with a documented “resources by version” catalog and many endpoint families (including sObject CRUD/search/query, composite, reports/analytics, etc.). The REST API reference provides a resources list and examples rooted at

name: HubSpot
researcher_notes:

- OAuth apps: “Once you’ve set up an OAuth backend, you can make API requests using the OAuth access token…” and “provided using the `Bearer` HTTP authorization header.” (same page as above): https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Static auth access tokens (single-account installs): also described on the same authentication overview page: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- Developer API keys (used for some features/endpoints) are passed as the `hapikey` query parameter: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview

2) **Self-serve vs gated credential access (free/trial vs paid/admin/approval)**
- You must set up an OAuth app if distributing to multiple accounts/Marketplace; HubSpot says OAuth is “required for multiple accounts” and requires hosting an OAuth backend: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/overview
- OAuth quickstart explicitly states: “You must be a Super Admin to install an app in a HubSpot account.”: https://developers.hubspot.com/docs/apps/developer-platform/build-apps/authentication/oauth/oauth-quickstart-guide

name: Pipedrive
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) (what the API uses)
- **API token authentication**: Pipedrive requires an `api_token` and it must be sent in the **`x-api-token` header** for requests (examples shown in docs). Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/core-api-concepts-authentication  
- **OAuth 2.0 authentication** (for marketplace/public apps): Pipedrive uses **OAuth 2.0** and expects API calls to include the user’s **`access_token`** in the **`Authorization: Bearer ...`** header. Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/marketplace-oauth-api and OAuth flow details (incl. HTTP Basic Auth for exchanging codes) at: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/marketplace-oauth-authorization  
- **OAuth token exchange uses HTTP Basic Auth**: when exchanging `authorization_code` for tokens, requests authenticate using **HTTP Basic Auth** with `client_id:client_secret`. Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/marketplace-oauth-authorization  

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- **API token is self-serve per user/company**: docs explain you can get your `api_token` from the Pipedrive web app under *Settings → Personal preferences → API* (and provides a page/link). Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/how-to-find-the-api-token  
- **OAuth credentials are self-serve but require a sandbox + app registration**:
  - Pipedrive says OAuth setup requires a **developer sandbox account** and registering an app in **Developer Hub** to obtain `client_id` / `client_secret`. Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/marketplace-oauth-api  
  - Developer Hub lets you “save a draft app and get your `client_id` and `client_secret`” (i.e., credential generation is available in the hub after sandbox access). Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/developer-hub  
  - The process for obtaining `client_id` / `client_secret` (“Create an app”, then in OAuth & access scopes) is described as part of Developer Hub. Source: https://pipedrive.readme.io/docs/client-id-and-client-secret

name: Zoho CRM
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the Zoho CRM API uses  
- Zoho CRM API v8 uses OAuth 2.0 for authentication, and the access token must be sent in the HTTP `Authorization` header as `Zoho-oauthtoken {access_token}`.  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/oauth-overview.html  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/access-refresh.html  
- Token issuance/flows: access token must be obtained from a grant token (authorization code) for the OAuth authorization-code flow; self-client grant tokens can be generated in Zoho’s API console.  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/auth-request.html  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/token-validity.html  

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access  
- You can register an OAuth client in Zoho’s Developer Console/API Console and then generate self-client grant tokens there (i.e., credentials are obtainable without contacting Zoho for a partnership—registration is the normal self-serve step).  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/register-client.html  
- The Zoho CRM docs explicitly describe different client types including “Self Client”, used for backend-only integrations, and note that you can generate the grant token from the API console.  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/register-client.html  
  - https://www.zoho.com/crm/developer/docs/api/v8/auth-request.html

1) Auth method(s) used by DealCloud API (from DealCloud docs)
- DealCloud uses OAuth2 **Client Credentials** to generate a bearer token for API calls; the token is sent in the `Authorization: Bearer {{token}}` HTTP header. Docs: https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs/token
- DealCloud also documents **API keys** for enabling/issuing access via site admin/user-group permissions, and those credentials are used together with the OAuth2 flow (the token doc references providing `client_id` and `client_secret` / `apiKey`-style values). Docs: https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs/apikeys and https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs/token

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- DealCloud’s docs indicate you must have the intended user assigned to a user group with appropriate **API** capability enabled under Admin → User Management (and if you can’t access Admin/API key enabling, you should contact your org admin or DealCloud Customer Success Manager). This implies credentials are typically **not fully self-serve** without admin/CSM involvement. Docs: https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs/apikeys
- DealCloud also states: “Get in touch with your account manager to have **Publications enabled on your site**” (capability gating beyond just token/key possession). Docs: https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs/publications/getting_started

- API surface style: **REST APIs** (the API docs explicitly describe REST API endpoints and that examples use HTTP; they also mention Swagger/OpenAPI testing). Docs: https://api.docs.dealcloud.com/docs
- Breadth / functional areas: the docs’ overview lists multiple functional API areas including:

name: Zendesk
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the Zendesk API uses  
- **OAuth access tokens (Bearer)**: Zendesk supports OAuth access tokens; requests use `Authorization: Bearer {access_token}`.  
  URL: https://developer.zendesk.com/api-reference/introduction/security-and-auth/  
- **API tokens (Basic auth)**: Zendesk supports API tokens (auto-generated passwords) using **Basic auth** with credentials formatted as `{email_address}/token:{api_token}` (base64-encoded and sent in the `Authorization` header).  
  URL: https://developer.zendesk.com/api-reference/introduction/security-and-auth/  
- **Messaging JWT (JWT)**: For Zendesk Messaging, Zendesk uses JWTs to identify end users (JWT signed by your backend with a secret from Admin Center).

name: Intercom
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the Intercom API uses  
- Intercom’s API supports **two auth modes**:  
  - **Access Tokens** for “private apps” accessing **your own workspace data**  
  - **OAuth** for “public apps” accessing **other people’s** Intercom data  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/build-an-integration/learn-more/authentication  
- Tokens are used via **`Authorization: Bearer <token>`** in API requests.  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/build-an-integration/learn-more/authentication  
- REST API bearer auth schema is explicitly documented as **`bearerAuth` (http, scheme: bearer)** in the OpenAPI reference.  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/references/rest-api/api.intercom.io.md  

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (trial / paid / approval)  
- **Access Tokens are self-serve**: Intercom says it “**provides you with an Access Token as soon as you create an app** on your workspace,” and it’s visible in the Developer Hub.  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/build-an-integration/learn-more/authentication  
- **OAuth credential access is gated/approved** for public integrations:  
  - Intercom requires OAuth for public apps that access other accounts.  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/build-an-integration/learn-more/authentication  
  - Intercom’s “Setting up OAuth” includes an approval process and describes applying for OAuth from a **TEST** app first and later applying for approval for the main production app.  
  Source: https://intercom-developer-hub.readme.io/docs/applying-for-oauth  
- Additional note: Intercom documents that **development workspaces are free and US-only**, while private-app development for production workspaces is tied to your own paid workspace.  
  Source: https://developers.intercom.com/docs/build-an-integration/getting-started

name: Freshdesk
researcher_notes:
Freshdesk (freshdesk.com) — AI-agent toolkit buildability research (API → MCP/actions)

1) Auth method(s) the API uses
- Freshdesk REST API uses **HTTP Basic Access Authorization**. The docs state you can authenticate using either your helpdesk username/password, or a **personal API key**; if you use the API key, **there is no need for any password** (dummy password acceptable).  
  Source: https://developer.freshdesk.com/api/v1/ (Authentication section; includes Basic auth + curl examples)  
- The docs also show the Authorization usage pattern with API key: `curl -v -u apikey:X ...` and that API key can be used in place of username.  
  Source: https://developer.freshdesk.com/api/v1/

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (trial / free / paid / admin approval)
- Freshdesk provides an **API key per agent** that can be retrieved via the Freshdesk UI by an admin/account admin; the support article notes API key visibility and that **administrator/account administrator privileges** are required to perform helpdesk activities using the API.  
  Source: https://support.freshdesk.com/support/solutions/articles/225435-where-can-i-find-my-api-key-
- However, the article explicitly notes a plan-based restriction: **“If your account is on the Sprout plan, the API key and the API functionality will NOT be available.”**  
  Source: https://support.freshdesk.com/support/solutions/articles/225435-where-can-i-find-my-api-key-
- For **MCP integration (Freshworks MCP EAP)** specifically, access is gated:
  - Applicable plans: **Freshdesk Enterprise**
  - MCP integration is in **Beta** and available only via an **Early Access Program (EAP) for selected customers**
  - To request access: contact TAM or email support@freshdesk.com
  - Access requirements: only **users with administrator and agent privileges**  
  Source: https://support.freshdesk.com/support/solutions/articles/50000012670-model-context-protocol-mcp-integration-in-freshdesk-eap-

Verdict on credential access: REST API

name: Front
researcher_notes:
1) **Auth method(s)**  
- Front’s API supports **OAuth 2.0 and API Tokens**. **Both are used via Bearer authentication** for API requests.  
  - API requests: `Authorization: Bearer [REQUEST_TOKEN]` (token in `Authorization` header prefixed by `Bearer`)  
  - OAuth token exchange uses **Basic authentication** to the OAuth token endpoint, then subsequent API calls use **Bearer**.  
  Sources:  
  - https://dev.frontapp.com/reference/introduction  
  - https://dev.frontapp.com/me/docs/authentication  
  - https://dev.frontapp.com/docs/oauth  

2) **Self-serve vs gated credential access**  
- **Self-serve for developer credentials**: you can create/managing apps and obtain OAuth/API token credentials in your Front **company Developers settings**, and **you must be a company admin** to access/manage these.  
  Sources:  
  - https://community.front.com/api-news-updates-40/create-and-manage-apps-directly-in-front-99 (states you no longer have to contact Front Support to obtain OAuth credentials; Developers page is in company settings and requires admin)  
  - https://dev.frontapp.com/me/docs/create-and-manage-apps (explicitly: “You must be a company admin to create or manage apps.”)  
- Also, Front’s docs on API tokens state: **“You must be a Front company admin to manage API tokens.”**  
  Sources:  
  - https://help.front.com/en/articles/2331  

3) **Public API surface breadth + style (and existing MCP server)**  
- **Style / surface**: Front’s platform docs describe multiple capabilities, with the main backend being the **Core API**. The Core API is a **JSON REST-style** API with a documented **base URL** (`https://api2.frontapp.com`) and supports JSON and multipart uploads for some endpoints.  
  Source: https://dev.frontapp.com/reference/introduction  
- **Breadth (high level)**: Front’s platform includes **Core API**, **Channel API**, and **Plugin SDK**, plus additional capabilities (webhooks mentioned in docs).  
  Source: https://dev.frontapp.com/docs/welcome

name: Plain
researcher_notes:
Plain (plain.com) appears well-suited to an “AI-agent toolkit” approach (either via an MCP server or agent-callable actions) because it already provides an MCP server that exposes substantial support workflows, and it also has an authenticated GraphQL API.

1) Auth method(s) used
- **GraphQL API (machine/integration access): Bearer API keys**
  - Plain’s GraphQL API uses `Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN` with the token being your API key.  
  URL: https://www.plain.com/docs/graphql/introduction
  - API keys are created under **Settings → Machine Users**, and then used as a Bearer token.  
  URL: https://www.plain.com/docs/graphql/authentication
  - Help-center confirmation of API key creation and usage header format:  
  URL: https://help.plain.com/article/creating-api-keys
- **MCP server (agent tooling access): OAuth**
  - Plain’s MCP server uses **OAuth** so the connection shares the same permissions as your existing Plain user; **no separate API keys needed**.  
  URL: https://help.plain.com/article/mcp-server
  - MCP setup explicitly says “Set authentication to OAuth” for creating the MCP app in ChatGPT.  
  URL: https://help.plain.com/article/mcp-server

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- **Self-serve for GraphQL API keys (via UI settings)**
  - Docs instruct you to create machine users and API keys directly in Plain settings, including fine-grained permissions, and copy the key.  
  URL: https://www.plain.com/docs/graphql/authentication
  - Help-center “Creating API Keys” describes the same self-serve flow under Settings → Machine Users.  
  URL: https://help.plain.com/article/creating-api-keys
- **MCP access: requires OAuth login, but may be gated by admin ability to add custom apps**
  - MCP server docs: you create/configure an app integration and authenticate via OAuth.  
  URL: https://help.plain.com/article/mcp-server
  - If you “don’t see Advanced Settings… you might need to reach out to your administrator to enable adding ‘Custom Apps’.”

Zoho Cliq (zoho.com/cliq) looks quite buildable as an “agent toolkit” because it has a full OAuth2-authenticated REST API (v2 + v3) plus an *official* Zoho MCP server for Cliq. Below is evidence-backed analysis of auth, credential access, API surface, MCP existence, and buildability.

  - https://www.zoho.com/cliq/help/restapi/v3/oauth/  
- API domain/root: **`https://cliq.zoho.com/api/v3`** (and v2 uses `https://cliq.zoho.com/api/v2`).

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access

  - https://www.zoho.com/cliq/help/restapi/v3/registerclient/

name: Telegram
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) used

**Bot API (simplified HTTP interface for bots)**
- You authenticate each API request using your **bot token embedded in the URL**: `https://api.telegram.org/bot<YOUR_BOT_TOKEN>/<METHOD_NAME>` (token is “unique” per bot).  
  Source: https://core.telegram.org/bots/tutorial (token + example `.../getMe`) and https://core.telegram.org/bots/api (Bot API overview).

**Telegram API / MTProto via TDLib (user-client / custom clients)**
- Authorization is based on **MTProto client login**, where you provide **api_id** and **api_hash** (obtained from https://my.telegram.org) and then complete login via verification code / other flows.  
  Source (obtaining credentials): https://core.telegram.org/api/obtaining_api_id  
  Source (login/verification via `auth.sendCode` + parameters): https://core.telegram.org/api/auth  

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access

**Bot API**
- Credentials are self-serve for developers: obtain a bot token by contacting **@BotFather** and issuing **/newbot**.  
  Source: https://core.telegram.org/bots/tutorial (section “Obtain Your Bot Token”)

**Telegram API / TDLib (user/client API)**
- Credentials (**api_id**, **api_hash**) are obtained via **https://my.telegram.org** (login + “API development tools” form).  
  Source: https://core.telegram.org/api/obtaining_api_id

**Buildability implication:** both major API paths use self-serve credential acquisition (no contact-sales step found in the core docs above).

name: Google Ads
researcher_notes:
Google Ads (developers.google.com/google-ads) as an AI-agent toolkit (MCP server / agent-callable actions) is **buildable today** via the **Google Ads API**, and there is even an **official Google Ads MCP server** already published.

1) Auth method(s)
- **OAuth 2.0 + access token**: Requests require `Authorization: Bearer YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN` (OAuth2 access token).  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/concepts/call-structure
- **Developer token header**: Every API call must include `developer-token: DEVELOPER_TOKEN`.  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/rest/auth
- **Manager/linked accounts header(s)**:
  - If acting as a **manager** for client accounts: include `login-customer-id`.  
    URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/rest/auth
  - For partner-linked account flows: `linked-customer-id` is used (docs describe linked-account behavior).  
    URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/rest/auth
- **OAuth credential acquisition options**: The REST auth docs state both **user OAuth flow** and **service account flow** are supported.  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/rest/auth

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- **Developer tokens are requested by applying in the “API Center”** inside a **Google Ads manager account**:  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/api-policy/developer-token
- The developer token access model is **graded**:
  - When you sign up, you may be granted **Explorer access** automatically, or **Test Account Access** if auto-review fails; to get higher access you must request **Basic or Standard** and complete additional steps (approval review).  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/api/docs/api-policy/developer-token
- Practical implication: you can start with **self-serve Test/Explorer**, but **production access is not guaranteed** and can require review/approval for Basic/Standard.  
  URL: https://developers.google.com/google-ads/a

BigCommerce (developer.bigcommerce.com) is buildable as an **AI-agent toolkit / MCP action set** because it has a rich authenticated API surface with clear auth flows and a documented MCP server endpoint. Below is evidence-backed research across the four requested areas.

  - BigCommerce documents a modified OAuth2 flow for single-click apps, including a callback endpoint (`GET /auth`) and exchanging an authorization code for an `access_token` via:

- **REST/GraphQL Admin API requests use stable tokens in `X-Auth-Token`**
  - “Most of our REST endpoints and GraphQL Admin API endpoints use the `X-Auth-Token` header” and the value is an OAuth-generated access token.
  - Source: https://developer-center-bigcommerce-dx.vercel.app/docs/start/authentication
- **Storefront GraphQL API uses a Bearer token**
  - GraphQL Storefront auth is via HTTP `Authorization: Bearer {token}`.
  - Source: https://docs.bigcommerce.com/developer/learn/courses/graphql-storefront-api/auth/authentication
- **Token generation for storefront APIs includes stateless “private” tokens**
  - Private tokens are created via:
    - `POST https://api.bigcommerce.com/stores/{store_hash}/v3/storefront/api-token-private`
  - Source: https://docs.bigcommerce.com/developer/api-reference/rest/admin/authentication-apis/storefront-api-tokens/private-api-token/create-private-token.md

name: Clay
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s)
- Clay “HTTP API” (in-app enrichment) authenticates to third-party APIs by **storing auth credentials at the workspace level** in an “HTTP API (Headers) account” and injecting them into requests (e.g., `Authorization: Bearer ...` or `X-API-KEY: ...`). Clay encrypts and stores the headers for reuse. Source: https://university.clay.com/docs/http-api-integration-overview
- Clay also supports **“HTTP API with JWT Authentication”**: Clay makes a **token fetch** call to your provider’s token endpoint using stored credentials, then injects the resulting JWT into each request’s `Authorization` header (and auto-refreshes ~every 55 minutes). Source: https://university.clay.com/docs/http-api-with-jwt-authentication-integration-overview

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access (trial/paid/admin/partner)
- Clay’s general guidance indicates its programmable access is not a “traditional public API”; instead, you interact via **webhooks**, **wrappers**, or an **Enterprise-only People & Company API**. Source: https://university.clay.com/docs/using-clay-as-an-api
- For the **Clay HTTP API enrichment feature itself**, the docs describe how to configure accounts/headers, but they do not state in the pages we found whether the feature is self-serve from specific free/trial tiers vs paid-gated. (So we can’t claim the exact plan gating level from the pages fetched.)
- For credentials used by the HTTP API enrichment *to call other services*, Clay provides the mechanism to store your own API keys / bearer tokens (i.e., you bring your own credentials) via `HTTP API (Headers)` or JWT account setup. Source: https://university.clay.com/docs/http-api-integration-overview and https://university.clay.com/docs/http-api-with-jwt-authentication-integration-overview

name: Supabase
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the Supabase API uses  
- Supabase projects accept a JWT in `Authorization: Bearer <token>`; the Supabase client library manages user session JWTs automatically. Supabase JWTs can also be derived from API keys via short-lived JWTs (“On-the-fly when using publishable or secret API keys”). Source: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/auth/jwts  
- For REST/Data API requests, Supabase requires an API key sent via the `apikey` header; the REST endpoint is `https://<project_ref>.supabase.co/rest/v1` and “both of these routes require the key to be passed through an `apikey` header.” Source: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/api/creating-routes  
- API keys: Supabase documents publishable keys vs secret keys, including that secret keys use the built-in `service_role` role and bypass RLS. Source: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/api-keys

- Supabase API keys are obtained from your Supabase project: “find all keys in the Settings > API Keys section of the Dashboard” (i.e., self-serve once you have a project). Source: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/getting-started/api-keys  
- The official hosted MCP server is OAuth-style / browser-authenticated: “a browser window opens where you log in to your Supabase account and grant the MCP client access.” Source: https://supabase.com/docs/guides/ai-tools/mcp

name: Neo4j
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s)
- Neo4j “driver-level” auth supports multiple schemes, including **basic (username/password)**, **bearer (base64-encoded token)**, **kerberos (base64-encoded ticket)**, **custom**, and **none** (if auth is disabled). Evidence:
  - https://neo4j.com/docs/api/java-driver/current/org.neo4j.driver/org/neo4j/driver/AuthTokens.html
- Neo4j Aura (Neo4j’s managed service) **Aura API uses OAuth 2.0**:
  - Bearer token in `Authorization: Bearer <access_token>` for API requests
  - Token endpoint: `POST https://api.neo4j.io/oauth/token`
  - Token endpoint auth uses **HTTP Basic Auth** with `client_id:client_secret`:
  - https://neo4j.com/docs/aura/api/authentication/

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- Aura API credentials are **self-serve**: “All tiers can create Aura API credentials” in the Aura console, and you obtain **Client ID** and **Client Secret**. Evidence:
  - https://neo4j.com/docs/aura/api/authentication/
- For the Neo4j MCP server, credential gating depends on your Neo4j deployment:
  - The MCP “HTTP transport” mode supports:
    - **Bearer token** auth (for Enterprise/Aura with SSO/OAuth)
    - **Basic auth** (username/password)
  - Evidence (explicitly describes both methods and where credentials come from):
    - https://github.com/neo4j-labs/neo4j-mcp-canary/blob/main/docs/CLIENT_SETUP.md
- For local MCP (STDIO mode), it requires providing `NEO4J_USERNAME` and `NEO4J_PASSWORD` via environment variables (so you must already have database credentials yourself; no evidence of any “external gating” by Neo4j from these docs):
  - https://github.com/neo4j-labs/neo4j-mcp-canary/blob/main/docs/CLIENT_SETUP.md

3) Public API surface breadth + existing MCP server(s)
- “Public API surface” for Neo4j depends on what you mean:
  - Neo4j provides official **language drivers** (Python/Java/.NET/etc.) where connections are made via **Bolt** (and related encrypted variants). Evidence (Python driver connects via Bolt; auth methods described there too)

name: Snowflake
researcher_notes:
Snowflake can be built as an **AI-agent toolkit** (MCP server / agent-callable actions) largely because it already has a **REST API** plus an **official managed MCP server** that exposes Snowflake tools with Snowflake-native auth + RBAC.

## 1) Auth method(s)
Snowflake REST APIs support multiple token types via `Authorization: Bearer ...` plus an optional header to declare the token type:

- **Key-pair auth (JWT)**: `Authorization: Bearer JWT` and optionally `X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: KEYPAIR_JWT` citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-rest-api/authentication  
- **OAuth**: `Authorization: Bearer oauth_token` and optionally `X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: OAUTH` citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-rest-api/authentication  
- **Programmatic Access Token (PAT)**: `Authorization: Bearer token_secret` and optionally `X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: PROGRAMMATIC_ACCESS_TOKEN` citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-rest-api/authentication  
- **Workload Identity Federation**: `Authorization: Bearer WIF.{provider}.{token}` and `X-Snowflake-Authorization-Token-Type: WORKLOAD_IDENTITY_FEDERATION` citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/developer-guide/snowflake-rest-api/authentication  

Programmatic access tokens can be used for several Snowflake endpoints (REST, SQL API, Catalog SDK, etc.) citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/user-guide/programmatic-access-tokens

## 2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
Snowflake’s docs describe how to **create programmatic access tokens** using SQL:

- `ALTER USER ... ADD PROGRAMMATIC ACCESS TOKEN` creates a token and returns `token_secret` in the command output citehttps://docs.snowflake.com/en/sql-reference/sql/alter-user-add-programmatic-access-token

name: Datadog
researcher_notes:
Datadog (docs.datadoghq.com/api) — AI agent toolkit buildability (MCP / agent-callable actions)

1) Auth method(s)
- Legacy/“API + application keys” auth:
  - All requests must be authenticated; writing requires an **API key**, and reading requires both **API key + application key**. Auth is via headers (API key header and sometimes application key header).  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/authentication/  
- API header names for the REST API:
  - “Authenticate … with an API key using header `DD-API-KEY` … for some endpoints … Application key … header `DD-APPLICATION-KEY`.”  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest.md  
- Scoped credential alternative (PAT/SAT) for API v2 calls:
  - Datadog supports Personal Access Tokens (PATs) and Service Access Tokens (SATs), which can be passed as **Bearer tokens** in the `Authorization` header (recommended), or via the `dd-application-key` header.  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/account_management/personal-access-tokens.md and https://docs.datadoghq.com/account_management/service-access-tokens.md
- Datadog MCP server auth:
  - Datadog MCP Server “uses OAuth 2.0 by default”, and if OAuth isn’t available it accepts **Datadog API key + application key** as `DD_API_KEY` and `DD_APPLICATION_KEY` HTTP headers.  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/mcp_server/setup.md

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- API keys / application keys are created from your Datadog org/account UI (“Manage your account’s API and application keys in Datadog”).  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/api/latest/authentication/ and https://docs.datadoghq.com/account_management/api-app-keys.md
- Application key secrets visibility is one-time depending on “One-Time Read (OTR) mode”:
  - Application key secrets are only displayed once when created, and cannot be retrieved later (OTR mode behavior described).  
  - Evidence: https://docs.datadoghq.com/account_management/api-app-keys.md

name: Notion
researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the API uses (Notion developer docs)
- Notion API requests require an HTTP **Bearer token** in the `Authorization` header: `Authorization: Bearer <token>` (and also `Notion-Version`). Source: https://developers.notion.com/reference/authentication
- Notion supports three token types/authorization flows:
  - **Internal connections** use a **static installation access token** (no OAuth flow). Source: https://developers.notion.com/guides/get-started/authorization and https://developers.notion.com/guides/get-started/internal-connections
  - **Personal access tokens (PATs)** are **user-scoped bearer tokens** created by a user in the Developer portal. Source: https://developers.notion.com/guides/get-started/personal-access-tokens
  - **Public connections** use **OAuth 2.0**. Source: https://developers.notion.com/guides/get-started/public-connections and OAuth token exchange endpoint: https://developers.notion.com/reference/create-a-token
- Notion’s **REST API base URL** is `https://api.notion.com`, following REST conventions. Source: https://developers.notion.com/reference/intro

name: Linear
researcher_notes:
LINEAR (linear.app) — agent-toolkit buildability research (evidence-backed)

1) Auth method(s)
- Linear’s public API is GraphQL at `https://api.linear.app/graphql` and supports **personal API keys** and **OAuth2**. Requests use an `Authorization` header.  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/developers/graphql
- OAuth2 flow details: auth URL uses `https://linear.app/oauth/authorize`; token endpoint is `https://api.linear.app/oauth/token`; access tokens are used as `Authorization: Bearer <ACCESS_TOKEN>`.  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/developers/oauth-2-0-authentication
- Actor authorization for agents: by adding `actor=app` to the OAuth authorization URL, mutations are performed “by the app” (useful for agent/service accounts).  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/developers/oauth-actor-authorization

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- **Personal API keys are self-serve for admins and (optionally) for members depending on workspace admin settings** (“Admins can choose whether or not Members can create their own API keys…”).  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/docs/api-and-webhooks
- For OAuth2, developers create an “OAuth2 application” configuration (client_id/client_secret) in Linear, which implies **workspace/integration setup inside the Linear workspace** (not “no-approval-needed” for customer workspaces). The docs recommend using a workspace for managing the OAuth2 Application because each admin user will have access.  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/developers/oauth-2-0-authentication
- (Related governance) Webhook/OAuth creation pages require admin permissions (“Creating and managing webhooks requires admin permissions… Create and manage webhooks and OAuth applications in Settings > Administration > API.”).  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/docs/api-and-webhooks

3) Public API surface breadth + existing MCP server
- API surface: **GraphQL-only** (no REST endpoints mentioned; everything goes through the GraphQL endpoint).  
  Evidence: https://linear.app/developer

name: Harvest
researcher_notes:
Harvest (help.getharvest.com/api-v2) buildability as an AI agent toolkit (MCP server / agent-callable actions)

1) Auth method(s) used by the Harvest API v2
- Harvest API v2 requires authenticating every request using either:
  - a **Personal Access Token (PAT)**, sent as `Authorization: Bearer $ACCESS_TOKEN`, plus `Harvest-Account-Id: $ACCOUNT_ID` and a `User-Agent` header, OR
  - an **OAuth2 flow** (Authorization Code for server-side apps; Implicit grant for client-side), with access tokens used as `Authorization: Bearer ...` and the same required headers.  
  Source: https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2/authentication-api/authentication/authentication/  
- Additionally, the API overview specifies supported HTTP methods `GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE`, and reiterates required auth + `User-Agent` header behavior.  
  Source: https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2/introduction/overview/general/

2) Self-serve vs gated credential access
- **Personal Access Tokens are self-serve**: “creating a Personal Access Token from the Developers section of Harvest ID” is documented.  
  Source: https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2/authentication-api/authentication/authentication/
- **OAuth2 credentials are also created in the Developers section of Harvest ID** (register an OAuth2 Application / OAuth2 Client there). The docs do not describe any “contact sales” gating, only that you must register the application and provide redirect URL + scope-related settings.  
  Source: https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2/authentication-api/authentication/authentication/
- However, OAuth2 itself is user-consent-based: the app must redirect the user to `https://id.getharvest.com/oauth2/authorize...` and the user authorizes access.  
  Source: https://help.getharvest.com/api-v2/authentication-api/authentication/authentication/

3) Public API surface + breadth + existing MCP server(s)

researcher_notes:
1) Auth method(s) the API uses  
- The **Devin MCP server** (`https://mcp.devin.ai/`) is an **authenticated service** that uses Devin API credentials, sent in the MCP client config via an HTTP header named `Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>` (and possibly `X-Org-Id` for enterprise). Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp  
- The **Devin API auth model** is “principal + token”, using **bearer tokens** in the `Authorization` header and **`cog_`-prefixed keys** for API v3. Source: https://docs.devin.ai/api-reference/authentication  
- Token types supported for the MCP server:  
  - Org-scoped **service user key** starting with `cog_` (no `X-Org-Id` needed)  
  - Enterprise service user key starting with `cog_` (**requires** `X-Org-Id`)  
  - Personal access token (PAT) starting with `cog_` (**requires** `X-Org-Id`)  
  - Legacy `apk_` / `apk_user_` keys are **not supported** by the Devin MCP server. Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp  
- For enterprise accounts, the MCP doc shows the exact header requirement: `X-Org-Id` plus `Authorization: Bearer <API_KEY>`. Source: https://docs.devin.ai/work-with-devin/devin-mcp

name: Grain
researcher_notes:
Grain (grain.com) looks buildable as an **AI-agent toolkit**, but *only* if you leverage its **first-party MCP server**. The underlying REST API exists, yet appears **partner-gated / beta-limited**, which is the biggest practical blocker for “agent-toolkit buildability” if you want to ship it as a general-purpose open integration.

## 1) Auth method(s)

### Grain public platform API (meeting data)
Grain’s developer docs describe multiple credential types, including **OAuth2 Authorization Code + PKCE** and **Personal/Workspace Access Tokens**, with requests authenticated via **`Authorization: Bearer TOKEN`** and versioned via **`Public-Api-Version`**:
- `Authorization: Bearer TOKEN` and token types (Personal Access Token, Workspace Access Token, OAuth2 Authorization Code + PKCE): https://developers.grain.com/  
- OAuth2 token + authorize flow details and version header + endpoint domain (`https://api.grain.com`): https://developers.grain.com/

Also documents:
- **Rate limit**: `300` requests per minute; `429 Too Many Requests`: https://developers.grain.com/

### Grain MCP server (agent-facing)
The (analytics) MCP server uses **OAuth 2.1** with scopes and returns MCP endpoint URL:
- Endpoint: `https://grainql.com/api/mcp`: https://docs.grainql.com/ai-tools/mcp/overview  
- Auth flow: Grain issues `mcpat_…` access token and `mcprt_…` refresh token; consent-based scopes: https://docs.grainql.com/ai-tools/mcp/overview
